title: Multifractal Signatures of Ageing and Dementia Development: A Multifractal Space-Filling Curve Analysis
authors: Marta Lotka, Jacek Grela, Zbigniew Drogosz, Jeremi K. Ochab, Paweł Oświęcimka
abstract: Multifractality is an effective formalism for quantifying the nonlinear, scale-free properties of complex data. In this study, we propose a novel and efficient methodology, termed Multifractal Space-filling Curve Analysis (MFSCA), for quantifying the correlation structure of multidimensional data. Within this framework, the original multidimensional data - while preserving both local and long-range organisational properties - are projected onto a one-dimensional representation using a fractal space-filling curve. The resulting one-dimensional signal is then analysed using multifractal algorithms. We demonstrate the utility of the method using both artificially generated multifractal structures and real data. In particular, we apply MFSCA to analyse magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) data from Alzheimer patients at different stages of dementia. Based on the results, we estimate the multifractal profiles of the brain for healthy subjects of different ages as well as for dementia patients. The analysis reveals that the spatial organization of brain structures, as measured by the degree of multifractality, progressively weakens with age and the development of dementia. A transition from multifractality to monofractality is observed both in control groups, when comparing the Young Control and Elderly Control groups, and among dementia subjects of similar age but at different stages of the disease, namely early dementia and mild cognitive impairment. Thus, from the perspective of multiscaling properties, the heterogeneous characteristics of spatial brain organization deteriorate under worsening conditions, leading to a homogeneous and weakly correlated structure. These findings not only effectively capture key aspects of brain organisation, but also demonstrate that the multifractality of MRI data can serve as a marker of structural brain changes.
summary: This paper introduces Multifractal Space-filling Curve Analysis (MFSCA) to analyze brain MRI scans across different stages of aging and dementia. By quantifying the degree of multifractality, the authors demonstrate that this complex spatial organization systematically degrades towards a simpler, monofractal structure as cognitive decline worsens. This suggests that multifractality serves as a quantitative biomarker for neurodegenerative processes.
main_problem: The study aims to quantify the nonlinear, scale-free properties of complex biological data, specifically analyzing how spatial organization in brain structures degrades during aging and dementia.
main_result: The analysis reveals a progressive weakening of multifractality—a transition from rich multifractal structure towards a homogeneous, monofractal state—as age increases and dementia progresses.
method: A novel Multifractal Space-filling Curve Analysis (MFSCA) is employed to project high-dimensional MRI data onto 1D time series, which are then analyzed using multifractal algorithms like MFDFA.
model_or_system: The system under study is human brain structure derived from T1-weighted Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) scans of subjects across various cognitive cohorts (Young Control through MCI).
key_observables: Multifractal profiles, generalized Hurst exponent h(q), multifractal spectrum width ($\Delta\alpha$), and asymmetry ($A$).
important_parameters: Age groups (e.g., Young vs. Elderly) and cognitive impairment stages (e.g., EMCI vs. MCI).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the loss of multifractality directly correlates with structural deterioration in brain organization, and uses techniques like IPTW to mitigate bias in observational comparisons.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate MFSCA flowcharts, group comparisons showing decreasing $\Delta\alpha$ and $H$ from young controls to dementia patients, and dimensionality reduction plots (t-SNE/LDA) separating the cognitive cohorts.
paper_structure: The paper introduces MFSCA for multidimensional data analysis, validates it using artificial cascades, applies it to OASIS-1 MRI data across age/dementia groups, quantifies metrics ($\Delta\alpha$, $A$), and uses LDA/t-SNE to show systematic progression along a continuum of disorder.
why_interesting: While the application is biological, the core methodology—quantifying complex non-linear correlations in high-dimensional data using fractal geometry—is mathematically rigorous and shares conceptual overlap with characterizing complexity in disordered quantum systems or neural network dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Flowchart of the Multifractal Space-filling Curve Analysis (MFSCA). A. Skull-stripped MRI volume is analyzed slice by slice along a given axis. B. Each slice is overlaid with a space-filling curve (Hilbert curve). A one-dimensional representation of the slice is formed by voxel intensity along the curve. C. Multifractal detrended fluctuation analysis, see eq. (5), provides the generalized Hurst exponent h(q) and the multifractal spectrum f(α). The Hurst exponent H = h(2), the width of the spectrum ∆α, and its asymmetry A are later used as features describing the slice. D. The multifractal features of all slices along a given axis are collected and plotted as brain profiles. Profiles...

FIG. 2: Mapping voxel intensity into 1D. (Top) First four iterations of Hilbert space-filling curves on a square grid. In a given slice of MRI volume, the final iteration of the curve sets the order of traversal of all voxel centers of that slice. (Middle) Visualization of the MRI volume’s slice. The 3D landscape illustrates voxel intensities in that slice. The intensities are mapped onto the positions of the voxels along the curve. (Bottom) Linearized slice, i.e., one-dimensional representation of voxel intensities along the Hilbert curve.

FIG. 3: MFSCA analysis of the deterministic cascades. a) Multifractal spectra obtained for the sample cascades with different degrees of complexity (width of the multifractal spectrum). b) Comparison of the estimated widths of the multifractal spectrum with its theoretical counterparts. c) comparison of the maxima localisation estimated spectra f(α) with theoretical counterparts. d) multifractal spectrum characteristics for the surrogate data - only linear correlations are preserved. Error bars denote the standard deviation over 10 realizations.
FIG. 4: (Top) Fluctuation functions for slice 129 along the y-axis (sagittal) for representative individuals from each cohort. (Bottom) Corresponding multifractal spectra.

FIG. 5: Slice-wise profiles of the long-scale Hurst exponent H for the Young, Middle-Aged, and Elderly Control cohorts in the OASIS-1 dataset. The panels show profiles along the x (top), y (middle), z (bottom) axes. In each panel, the cohort median H value at each slice is indicated by a dot, and neighbouring points are connected to highlight the profile shape. The shaded bands indicate the 95% bootstrap confidence interval of the median (nboot = 5000). The labels inside the panels indicate the anatomical orientation of the slice index.
